bullrun1.caption: Sudley Ford on Bull Run, where McDowell's flanking column crossed to open the first great battle of the war in July 1861 — a Union rout that taught the North the war would be long.
bullrun1.alt: A wartime photograph of Sudley Ford on Bull Run near Manassas, Virginia, with a stone ford crossing, a soldier, a wagon, and a house on the hill beyond.
bullrun1.periodNote: A wartime Library-of-Congress photograph of Sudley Ford, the crossing of McDowell's flank column at First Bull Run.
bullrun1.file: File:Sudley Ford and Church, Bull Run, where General McDowell crossed with Hunter's column to turn the extreme left of the enemy's position LCCN2016651686.jpg
bullrun1.artist: Barnard, George N., 1819-1902, photographer; Barnard & Gibson, copyright claimant
bullrun1.credit: Library of Congress Catalog: http://lccn.loc.gov/2016651686 Image download: http://cdn.loc.gov/service/pnp/ppmsca/49600/49627v.jpg Original url: https://www.loc

antietam.caption: Confederate dead along the Sunken Road ("Bloody Lane") at Antietam, photographed by Alexander Gardner days after September 17, 1862 — the bloodiest day in American history, and among the first photographs of the American dead ever shown to the public.
antietam.alt: A wartime photograph of Confederate soldiers lying dead along the Sunken Road at the Antietam battlefield.
antietam.periodNote: Alexander Gardner / James Gibson, "The Dead of Antietam," September 1862 — genuine field photographs of the battle's aftermath.
antietam.credit: File from The Photographic History of The Civil War in Ten Volumes: Volume Two, Two Years of Grim War. The Review of Reviews Co., New York. 1911. p. 74.

fredericksburg.caption: Union pontoon bridges across the Rappahannock at Fredericksburg, December 1862 — Burnside's army crossed here to be slaughtered in futile charges against the stone wall at Marye's Heights.
fredericksburg.alt: A wartime photograph of Union pontoon bridges spanning the Rappahannock River at Fredericksburg, Virginia.
fredericksburg.periodNote: Photographed during the Fredericksburg operations, 1862-63 (A.J. Russell and others, U.S. Military Railroad).
fredericksburg.file: File:Pontoon bridge across the Rappahannock - negative by T.H. O'Sullivan; positive by A. Gardner. LCCN2006685381.jpg
fredericksburg.artist: O'Sullivan, Timothy H., 1840-1882, photographer; Gardner, Alexander, 1821-1882, photographer
fredericksburg.credit: Library of Congress Catalog: https://lccn.loc.gov/2006685381 Image download: https://cdn.loc.gov/service/pnp/ppmsca/12500/12553v.jpg Original url: https://www.l

chancellorsville.caption: Confederate dead behind the stone wall at Marye's Heights, May 3, 1863 — Sedgwick's corps stormed the heights at Second Fredericksburg during the Chancellorsville campaign, taking the ground that had been a slaughter-pen five months before.
chancellorsville.alt: A wartime photograph of Confederate dead lying behind the stone wall at the foot of Marye's Heights, Fredericksburg.
chancellorsville.periodNote: Andrew J. Russell, May 3, 1863 — taken during the Chancellorsville campaign (Second Battle of Fredericksburg).
chancellorsville.artist: Photographed by Capt. en:Andrew J. Russell
chancellorsville.credit: This tag does not indicate the copyright status of the attached work. A normal copyright tag is still required. See Commons:Licensing.

vicksburg.caption: The Shirley House amid the Union siege works at Vicksburg, 1863 — Grant's army dug in around the city until its July 4 surrender split the Confederacy along the Mississippi.
vicksburg.alt: A wartime photograph of the Shirley House surrounded by the dug-in Union siege lines at Vicksburg, Mississippi.
vicksburg.periodNote: Photographed during the 1863 siege of Vicksburg; the Shirley House ("the white house") stood amid the 45th Illinois' encampment.
vicksburg.file: File:Quarters of Logan's Division in the trenches in front of Vicksburg LCCN2013649023.jpg
vicksburg.credit: Library of Congress Catalog: http://lccn.loc.gov/2013649023 Image download: https://cdn.loc.gov/service/pnp/cph/3a10000/3a14000/3a14200/3a14259r.jpg Original ur

gettysburg.caption: Timothy O'Sullivan's "A Harvest of Death" — the dead on the field at Gettysburg, July 1863. The war's turning point cost some 50,000 casualties in three days.
gettysburg.alt: A wartime photograph of soldiers lying dead in a field at Gettysburg, Pennsylvania, in July 1863.
gettysburg.periodNote: Timothy H. O'Sullivan, "A Harvest of Death," July 1863 — published by Alexander Gardner; a genuine field photograph of the dead.
gettysburg.file: File:Incidents of the War A Harvest of Death.jpg

_imageless.malvern: No clean PD wartime photograph of the Malvern Hill field exists on Commons — only Sneden watercolours, Leslie's/Harper's lithographs, "barrage"/"gunboat" sketches, and battle maps. The Seven Days fields (July 1862) were not photographed during the fighting.
_imageless.chickamauga: No clean PD wartime photograph of the Chickamauga field exists on Commons — only battle maps (McElroy/Ohio Commission) and postwar monument photos. The dense Georgia woods were not photographed during the Sept 1863 fighting.
_imageless.shiloh: No clean PD wartime photograph of the Shiloh field / Pittsburg Landing exists on Commons — the candidates were a 1917 "Birdseye View" drawn panorama and a book ENGRAVING of the steamers at the landing ("from a photograph taken a few days after the battle", but reproduced as a woodcut, not the photo). Caught on VIEW. Shiloh (April 1862) was not cleanly photographed during/just after the fighting.
